walkthrough.title: Sort the Cards by Age
walkthrough.text: Let's get Innovation set up. Open the box and find all 105 cards. Each card has an age number from 1 to 10 on the back. Sort them into ten separate piles by age number. You should have roughly ten or eleven cards in each pile. These are your supply piles, and they represent the march of civilization from Prehistory all the way to the Information age.

walkthrough.title: Arrange the Supply Piles
walkthrough.text: Lay the ten supply piles out in a row or arc in the center of the table, ordered from age 1 on the left to age 10 on the right. Make sure everyone can see and reach them. Each pile should be face down so nobody can see which specific cards are in each age. During the game, you will draw from these piles to get new cards.

walkthrough.title: Set Aside Normal Achievements
walkthrough.text: Now, without looking at them, randomly remove one card from each of the age 1 through age 9 supply piles. That is nine cards total. Place these face down in a row near the supply piles. These are the normal achievements. They are numbered 1 through 9 and represent milestones your civilization can claim during the game. Do not remove a card from the age 10 pile. There is no age 10 achievement.

walkthrough.title: Place the Special Achievements
walkthrough.text: Find the five special achievement cards. These have unique names rather than age numbers: Monument, Empire, World, Wonder, and Universe. Place all five face up near the normal achievements where everyone can see them. Unlike normal achievements, special achievements are claimed immediately when you meet their conditions during play. They are always available until someone claims them.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Player Areas
walkthrough.text: Each player needs space in front of them for three things. First, a board area where you will play cards in up to five color stacks. Second, a score pile area where you will tuck scored cards face down. Third, a hand area for cards you have drawn but not yet played. You might also want a reference card if one came in your box. Keep some open table space because your board can spread out as you splay cards.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Cards
walkthrough.text: Each player draws two cards from the age 1 supply pile. Look at your two cards but keep them hidden from other players. These are both age 1 innovations from the dawn of civilization. You are about to make your first decision.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your First Innovation
walkthrough.text: Everyone simultaneously picks one of their two cards to meld as the first card on their board. Place it face up in front of you. The other card becomes your starting hand. This is a simultaneous reveal, so everyone flips their chosen card at the same time. Your melded card is your civilization's first innovation, and its icons and dogma effects will guide your opening strategy.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Look at the card each player melded. The player whose card title comes first alphabetically goes first. For example, Archery comes before The Wheel. In a four-player game, the first two players take only one action on their first turn instead of the normal two. In a two- or three-player game, only the very first player takes one action on their opening turn. After that, everyone takes two actions per turn as normal. You are ready to play.

summary.title: Sort the Cards by Age
bullets: 105 cards total, sorted into 10 supply piles by age (1-10)
Each pile has roughly 10-11 cards

summary.title: Arrange the Supply Piles
bullets: Lay piles in order from age 1 to age 10, face down
Place centrally where all players can reach

summary.title: Set Aside Normal Achievements
bullets: Remove 1 random card from each of ages 1-9 (9 cards total)
Place face down as achievement cards — do not look at them
No achievement for age 10

summary.title: Place the Special Achievements
bullets: 5 special achievements: Monument, Empire, World, Wonder, Universe
Place face up near normal achievements
Claimed immediately when conditions are met during play

summary.title: Set Up Player Areas
bullets: Board area: up to 5 color stacks for melded cards
Score pile: face-down cards worth points equal to their age
Hand area: drawn cards not yet played

summary.title: Deal Starting Cards
bullets: Each player draws 2 cards from the age 1 supply pile
Look at your cards privately

summary.title: Choose Your First Innovation
bullets: Simultaneously choose 1 card to meld face up on your board
The other card becomes your starting hand
Everyone reveals at the same time

summary.title: Determine First Player
bullets: Player whose melded card is first alphabetically goes first
First player (or first two in 4p) takes only 1 action on opening turn
All subsequent turns: 2 actions per turn

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure — Two Actions Per Turn
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you must take exactly two actions. You can pick any combination of the four action types, and you can even take the same action twice. The four actions are Draw, Meld, Dogma, and Achieve. After you finish both actions, play passes clockwise to the next player. That is the entire turn structure. Simple, right? The complexity comes from what each action does.

walkthrough.title: Action: Draw
walkthrough.text: When you take a Draw action, you add a card to your hand from the supply piles. Here is how you determine which pile to draw from: look at the highest age number among your top cards on your board. Draw from that age's supply pile. For example, if your highest top card is age 3, you draw from the age 3 pile. If you have no cards on your board at all, draw from age 1. The drawn card goes into your hand, where it waits to be melded or used by dogma effects later.

walkthrough.title: Empty Supply Piles
walkthrough.text: If the supply pile you need to draw from is empty, draw from the next higher non-empty pile instead. This applies to all draws in the game, not just the Draw action. For example, if you need to draw a 4 but the age 4, 5, and 6 piles are all empty, you draw from age 7. This is important: if you ever need to draw a card higher than age 10, meaning even the age 10 pile is empty, the game ends immediately. This is one of the two ways the game can end.

walkthrough.title: Action: Meld
walkthrough.text: When you take a Meld action, choose any card from your hand and place it face up on your board in the matching color stack. Every card has one of five colors: red, blue, green, yellow, or purple. If you already have cards of that color, the new card goes on top, covering the old one. The old card is not gone, it is just underneath. If that color was already splayed in a direction, the new card continues the splay. You can meld any card regardless of its age, even if it is much higher or lower than your current top card of that color.

walkthrough.title: Action: Achieve
walkthrough.text: When you take an Achieve action, you claim one of the available normal achievements. You must meet two requirements. First, your total score must be at least five times the age number of the achievement. So to claim the age 3 achievement, you need at least 15 points. Second, you must have a top card on your board with an age equal to or higher than the achievement. So for the age 3 achievement, you need a top card that is age 3 or higher. Important: achieving does not spend your points. They stay in your score pile. You just need to meet the threshold. Once claimed, an achievement can never be taken away from you.

walkthrough.title: Action: Dogma — The Core Mechanic
walkthrough.text: Dogma is the heart of Innovation. When you take a Dogma action, choose one of your top cards to activate. Each card has one or more dogma effects listed on it, and each effect has a featured icon shown as a small symbol next to it. Before resolving the effects, every player counts how many of that featured icon they have visible on their entire board. This count matters for two things: who shares your non-demand effects, and who is immune to your demand effects.

walkthrough.title: Dogma: Demand Effects
walkthrough.text: Some dogma effects are demands. They appear in a darker box and always start with the words 'I demand.' These are attacks against other players. When you activate a demand, read it aloud. Going clockwise from you, each opponent who has fewer of the featured icon than you must follow the demand's instructions. Players who have equal or more of the featured icon than you are immune and skip the demand entirely. If an opponent cannot fully carry out a demand, they do as much as they can.

walkthrough.title: Dogma: Non-Demand Effects (Sharing)
walkthrough.text: Non-demand effects are the cooperative part of dogma. Before you execute a non-demand effect, each opponent who has at least as many of the featured icon as you also gets to use it. They go clockwise starting from the player to your left, and then you go last. This is called sharing. Here is the key bonus: if any opponent actually shared one of your non-demand effects and it caused something in the game to change, you get a single free Draw action at the end of your Dogma action. A change means any cards were drawn, melded, tucked, scored, splayed, exchanged, revealed, or moved.

walkthrough.title: Dogma: Multiple Effects and Icon Counting
walkthrough.text: Many cards have more than one dogma effect. You resolve all of them in order with a single Dogma action. Do not recount icons between effects. The icon count at the start of the action is what matters for the entire action. Complete one effect fully, including all sharing, before moving on to the next effect. You are allowed to dogma a card even if nothing will happen. Sometimes that is a legitimate strategic choice.

walkthrough.title: The Six Icons
walkthrough.text: There are six different icon types in Innovation: crown, leaf, lightbulb, castle, factory, and clock. Each card has icons in up to four positions: three around the edges and one that is a large card image with no gameplay benefit. Icons matter for dogma sharing and immunity, and many dogma effects reference specific icons. The icons that appear depend on the age of the cards. Crowns and castles appear on early age cards, while factories and clocks show up in later ages. Accumulating the right icons through splaying is a major strategic consideration.

walkthrough.title: Splaying — Revealing Hidden Icons
walkthrough.text: Splaying is one of the most important mechanics in Innovation. When a dogma effect tells you to splay a color, you fan out the cards in that stack in a specific direction. Splaying left reveals one extra icon per covered card. Splaying right reveals two extra icons per covered card. Splaying up reveals three extra icons per covered card. If a color is already splayed and a new splay is applied, unsplay it first, then splay in the new direction. A stack with zero or one cards is always considered unsplayed. If a splayed stack gets reduced to one or zero cards, it loses its splay.

walkthrough.title: Scoring and Your Score Pile
walkthrough.text: Your score pile is a collection of face-down cards. Each card in your score pile is worth points equal to its age number. So an age 5 card is worth 5 points, and an age 1 card is worth 1 point. Your total score is the sum of all the age values in your score pile. You accumulate score primarily through dogma effects that instruct you to 'score' a card. Cards in your score pile are not on your board and have no icons or effects. You can always look at and count your own score pile.

walkthrough.title: The Five Card Colors
walkthrough.text: Every card is one of five colors: red, blue, green, yellow, or purple. When you meld a card, it goes on the stack of its matching color. Your board can have up to five stacks, one per color. Only the top card of each color can be activated with a Dogma action. Covered cards still contribute visible icons if the stack is splayed. Many dogma effects reference colors specifically, like 'splay your purple cards left' or 'return a card from your green stack.'

walkthrough.title: Keywords: Tuck, Return, Score, and Exchange
walkthrough.text: Several keywords come up frequently in dogma effects. Tuck means place a card on the bottom of its matching color stack on your board, continuing any existing splay. Return means place a card at the bottom of its matching supply pile, face down. Score means place a card face down in your score pile. Exchange means swap cards between two specified locations, even if one side is empty. Draw and Meld, Draw and Tuck, and Draw and Score are compound instructions where you draw a card and immediately do the second action with it.

walkthrough.title: Special Achievements — Conditions to Claim
walkthrough.text: The five special achievements are claimed immediately when you meet their condition, even in the middle of resolving a dogma effect. Monument: tuck six cards or score six cards during a single turn. Transferred cards from opponents do not count, nor does exchanging. Empire: have three or more visible icons of all six types on your board. World: have twelve or more clock icons visible on your board. Wonder: have five colors on your board, each splayed either up or right. Universe: have five top cards, each of value 8 or higher. Once claimed, special achievements are permanent.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The primary way to win is through achievements. The number you need depends on player count: 6 achievements in a two-player or team game, 5 achievements in a three-player game, and 4 achievements in a four-player game. These can be any mix of normal and special achievements. The moment you claim your final needed achievement, you win immediately. If nobody has won and a card higher than age 10 must be drawn, the game ends. In that case, the player with the highest score wins. If tied on score, most achievements wins. If still tied, it is a draw.

walkthrough.title: Team Rules for Four Players
walkthrough.text: With four players, you can play in two teams of two, seated across from each other. In team play, demand effects do not target your teammate. If you share a non-demand effect only with your teammate, you do not get a free draw. Any dogma effect that says 'opponent' does not affect your teammate. To win, your team needs 6 combined achievements, but each player claims achievements using only their own score. If the game ends by drawing past age 10, the team with the highest combined score wins.

walkthrough.title: Information Rules
walkthrough.text: You can always look at your own hand, score pile, and all cards on your board, including covered ones. You can always see the number of cards and their ages in any supply pile, any hand, and any score pile. Normal achievement identities are secret, even from the player who claimed them. Your group should decide at the start whether covered cards on opponents' boards and the quantity of cards in unsplayed opponent stacks are public or private information.

walkthrough.title: Welcome — Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted opening with three players: Alice, Bob, and Carol. Each will take different actions so you can see how Innovation works in practice. One player will make some common mistakes along the way, and we will learn from them. Ready? Let's set up and play.

walkthrough.title: Starting Setup
walkthrough.text: We have sorted the 105 cards into ten supply piles, removed nine random cards for normal achievements, and placed the five special achievements face up. Each player draws two age 1 cards. Alice gets Archery and The Wheel. Bob gets Clothing and Writing. Carol gets Metalworking and City States. Everyone secretly picks one card to meld.

walkthrough.title: Simultaneous Meld
walkthrough.text: Everyone reveals their choice. Alice melds Archery, a red card. Bob melds Clothing, a green card. Carol melds City States, a purple card. The remaining cards go into each player's hand: Alice keeps The Wheel, Bob keeps Writing, Carol keeps Metalworking. Alphabetically, Archery comes first, so Alice goes first. Since this is a 3-player game, only Alice takes one action on her first turn.

walkthrough.title: Alice's First Turn — One Action: Meld
walkthrough.text: Alice has only one action this turn because she is the first player. She looks at The Wheel in her hand. It is a green card with a useful non-demand dogma effect. She melds The Wheel onto her board. Now Alice has two top cards: Archery (red) and The Wheel (green). Her hand is empty, but she has set up two dogma options for future turns.

walkthrough.title: Bob's First Turn — Two Actions
walkthrough.text: Bob gets two actions. For his first action, Bob melds Writing from his hand. Writing is a blue card whose dogma effect lets you draw a 2. Now Bob has Clothing (green) and Writing (blue) on his board. For his second action, Bob dogmas Writing. Writing's featured icon is the lightbulb. Bob counts his lightbulbs: he has two. Alice has one, Carol has one. Since nobody has as many lightbulbs as Bob, nobody shares. Bob draws a 2 by himself. No one shared, so no free draw for Bob either.

walkthrough.title: Why Bob's Play Was Solid
walkthrough.text: Bob melded Writing to set up a useful dogma, then immediately activated it. By having more lightbulbs than his opponents, he kept the benefit to himself. Drawing an age 2 card gives Bob access to more advanced technology before anyone else. This meld-then-dogma pattern is one of the most common and effective sequences in Innovation.

walkthrough.title: Carol's First Turn — Action 1: Dogma
walkthrough.text: Carol wants to use City States. Its demand effect says: 'I demand you transfer a top card on your board of a different color from any card on my board to my score pile, if you have at least four on your board.' Carol reads this aloud. But wait: neither Alice nor Bob has four castles on their board. In fact, nobody has four of any icon this early in the game. The demand fizzles. Nothing happens.

walkthrough.title: Carol's Mistake: Wasting an Action on a Fizzled Demand
walkthrough.text: Carol just burned an action on a demand that could not possibly work. Early in the game, nobody has enough icons for most demands to trigger. Before you dogma a demand card, check whether any opponent actually meets the condition. If nobody has four castles, that demand is dead in the water. Carol would have been much better off drawing or melding.

walkthrough.title: Carol's First Turn — Action 2: Draw
walkthrough.text: For her second action, Carol takes a Draw. Her highest top card is age 1, so she draws from the age 1 pile. She gets a new age 1 card. Not the most exciting draw, but at least she has something to work with next turn. Her hand now has Metalworking plus this new card.

walkthrough.title: Alice's Second Turn — Action 1: Dogma Archery
walkthrough.text: Alice dogmas Archery. It is a demand card with the castle icon as its featured icon. Alice counts her castles: she has two. Bob has one, Carol has one. Both have fewer, so both must follow the demand. Archery says: 'I demand you draw a 1, then transfer the highest card in your hand to my hand.' Bob draws a 1, then must give Alice his highest card. Carol does the same. Alice just stole two cards and forced both opponents to help her. This is the power of demand effects.

walkthrough.title: Alice's Second Turn — Action 2: Meld
walkthrough.text: Alice now has several cards in hand from the Archery steal. She picks the best one and melds it. Let's say she melds an age 2 card. This is great because now her highest top card is age 2, which means her future draws come from the age 2 pile instead of age 1. She is accelerating through the ages faster than her opponents.

walkthrough.title: Bob's Second Turn — Action 1: Meld an Age 2 Card
walkthrough.text: Bob drew an age 2 card earlier from Writing's dogma. He melds it now. The new card covers an existing color stack if they match, or starts a new color if they do not. Bob now has an age 2 top card as well. He is keeping pace with Alice.

walkthrough.title: Bob's Second Turn — Action 2: Dogma the Age 2 Card
walkthrough.text: Bob dogmas his new age 2 card. Let's say it has a non-demand effect with a leaf icon. Bob counts leaves: he has two. Alice has one, Carol has two. Carol has equal leaves, so Carol gets to share the effect and goes first. Carol uses the effect, then Bob uses it. Because Carol shared and something changed, Bob gets a free draw. Bob draws from the age 2 pile because his highest top card is age 2. That free draw is a nice bonus.

walkthrough.title: Carol's Second Turn — Mistake: Ignoring Achievements
walkthrough.text: Carol has managed to score a couple of cards through effects, and her score is now 6 points. She also has an age 1 top card. That means she could claim the age 1 achievement, which requires only 5 points and a top card of age 1 or higher. But Carol does not think about achieving. Instead, she draws and melds. She has missed an opportunity to lock in an achievement that could be taken by someone else next turn.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Check If You Can Achieve
walkthrough.text: This is one of the most common beginner mistakes. Achievements are the primary way to win, and they are first-come-first-served. Once an achievement is gone, it is gone. Before each turn, quickly check your score and your highest top card. If you can afford an achievement, strongly consider claiming it. Your points might not last. An opponent's demand could steal cards from your score pile, or you might need to spend an action on something else. Grab achievements when you can.

walkthrough.title: Alice's Third Turn — Achieve and Draw
walkthrough.text: Alice checks her score. She has 7 points and an age 2 top card. She can claim the age 1 achievement, which requires 5 points and a top card of age 1 or higher. She takes the Achieve action and claims it. That achievement is now permanently hers and counts toward her victory condition. For her second action, she draws from age 2. Alice is building a lead.

walkthrough.title: Carol's Mistake: Not Understanding Sharing
walkthrough.text: Carol has a card with a non-demand dogma effect and wants to use it. The featured icon is the crown. Carol has one crown. Bob has two crowns. Carol activates the dogma. Since Bob has more crowns than Carol, he gets to share and goes first. Bob benefits from the effect, then Carol benefits. But because Bob shared and caused a change, Carol gets a free draw. Carol is upset that Bob got to use her card. But actually, the free draw she earned makes it worth it. New players often avoid dogma-ing when opponents will share, but that free draw is very valuable.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Sharing Is Not Always Bad
walkthrough.text: Do not be afraid to activate dogma effects just because opponents will share. Yes, they get the benefit too. But if any of them cause a change, you earn a free Draw action. That free draw comes from a higher age pile if you have high-value top cards, and it is essentially a third action on your turn. Sometimes sharing a modest effect with opponents is worth it purely for the free draw. Only avoid sharing when the opponent will benefit far more than you, or when you have a better alternative.

walkthrough.title: Watching Splays Develop
walkthrough.text: A few turns later, Bob activates a card that says 'You may splay your purple cards left.' Bob has three purple cards stacked up. He fans them to the left, revealing one extra icon on each covered card. Suddenly Bob has three more icons visible on his board. This makes his demands stronger and gives him better defense against other players' demands. Splaying is how you accumulate icon power in Innovation.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: Play continues with each player taking two actions per turn. You will draw higher-age cards as your top cards advance, meld them to upgrade your board, activate dogma effects to score points and attack opponents, and claim achievements whenever you can. The game accelerates as ages increase. Age 9 and 10 cards are extremely powerful and can end the game abruptly. Keep pushing for achievements before the late game gets out of hand.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen all four actions in practice, watched how demand effects and sharing work, and learned from three common mistakes: wasting actions on impossible demands, forgetting to check for achievements, and avoiding dogma just because opponents will share. You have the tools to play a full game of Innovation now.

walkthrough.title: Before You Innovate
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and common pitfalls to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all the keywords, icons, ages, and special achievements.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

bullets: MISTAKE: Carol avoids dogma-ing because Bob will share
But sharing earns Carol a free draw — effectively a 3rd action
Free draws from sharing are extremely valuable

summary.title: Lesson: Sharing Is Not Always Bad
bullets: Sharing triggers a free draw for you if anything changed
Free draw pulls from your highest top card age
Only avoid sharing when opponent benefits far more than you

summary.title: Watching Splays Develop
bullets: Bob splays purple left, revealing 1 extra icon per covered card
Three new visible icons strengthen his dogma position
Splaying is the main way to accumulate icon power

summary.title: Continue Playing
bullets: Draw, Meld, Dogma, Achieve — repeat with 2 actions per turn
Cards get more powerful as ages increase
Age 9-10 cards can end the game abruptly

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You have practiced all 4 actions
Three mistakes covered: fizzled demands, missed achievements, avoiding sharing
You are ready for a full game

summary.title: Before You Innovate
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy basics
Reference the Appendix for keywords, icons, and special achievements
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Always Check for Achievements
walkthrough.text: This is the single most important habit in Innovation. Before you plan your two actions each turn, quickly check: what is your score, and can you claim any available achievement? Achievements are the primary way to win, and they are first-come-first-served. If you can achieve, do it. Your points might evaporate next turn from a demand effect, or another player might take that achievement before your next turn. The only exceptions are very rare cases where another action is so game-changing that it outweighs the achievement. But ninety percent of the time, achieve if you can.

walkthrough.title: Meld-Then-Dogma Is Your Bread and Butter
walkthrough.text: The most common and effective two-action pattern in Innovation is to meld a card, then immediately dogma it. You place a new innovation on your board, then activate its effect right away. This gives you the element of surprise because opponents have not had a chance to react to the new card. It also lets you take advantage of effects that might benefit opponents if you wait — by the time they see the card, it has already done its work. Look for this pattern every turn.

walkthrough.title: Cover Cards Strategically
walkthrough.text: When you meld a card, it covers whatever was underneath in that color. This means you lose access to the old card's dogma effect but gain the new one. Before melding, ask yourself: am I giving up something more useful than I am gaining? Sometimes an age 1 card with a strong demand is more valuable than an age 4 card with a mediocre effect. Also pay attention to which icons the old card was contributing. Covering a card with two useful icons can reduce your icon counts and make you vulnerable to demands.

walkthrough.title: Pay Attention to Icon Counts
walkthrough.text: Icons are your shield and your sword. High icon counts protect you from demand effects and let your demands hit opponents. Low icon counts leave you vulnerable. At the start of the game, everyone has similar icon counts, so demands hit most people. As the game progresses and splaying kicks in, icon counts diverge wildly. Try to keep your icon counts competitive, especially for icons that appear on threatening demand cards on opponents' boards. Splaying is the fastest way to boost your icon counts.

walkthrough.title: Splay Whenever You Can
walkthrough.text: Splaying is enormously powerful. Even splaying left, which only reveals one extra icon per card, can double or triple your icon count in that type. Splaying right or up is even better. Whenever a dogma effect offers you the chance to splay, take it seriously. Even if the immediate benefit seems small, the icon boost compounds over time. A splayed stack of four or five cards is a fortress that protects you from demands and lets you dominate the icon economy.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Hoard Cards in Your Hand
walkthrough.text: Cards in your hand do nothing. They have no icons, no effects, and score no points. The only reason to keep cards in hand is if you plan to meld them soon or if you are saving them from being stolen by demands. In general, if you have more than two or three cards in hand, you are probably holding too many. Meld them to boost your board, or find a way to score them. A card on your board or in your score pile is always more useful than a card sitting in your hand.

walkthrough.title: Advance Through the Ages
walkthrough.text: Higher-age cards are generally more powerful. They have stronger dogma effects and are worth more points when scored. Your draw level is determined by your highest top card, so melding higher-age cards gives you access to better supply piles. Try to stay at or near the highest age among all players. If you fall behind in age, you will be drawing weaker cards while opponents get powerful ones. Conversely, if you can race ahead in age, you get first pick of the strongest innovations.

walkthrough.title: Respect the Late Game
walkthrough.text: Age 9 and 10 cards are game-changers. Some can win the game outright or completely reset the board state. Cards like Fission can remove all cards in play from the game. Others let you win instantly if you meet certain conditions. If you have a comfortable lead in achievements, try to close out the game before reaching this volatile stage. If you are behind, racing to age 9 and 10 may be your only shot at a comeback. Also remember: if the age 10 pile runs out and someone needs to draw higher, the game ends on score. Forcing this outcome can be a valid strategy if you have a high score but fewer achievements.

summary.title: Always Check for Achievements
bullets: Before planning actions, check if you can achieve
Achievements are first-come-first-served — grab them before opponents
Your points can disappear from demands; achievements are permanent
Achieve if you can, 90% of the time it is correct

bullets: Meld a card, then immediately dogma it = surprise + efficiency
Opponents cannot react to the new card before it activates
Look for this pattern every turn

A strong age 1 demand can be better than a mediocre age 4 effect
Watch which icons you lose when you cover a card

summary.title: Pay Attention to Icon Counts
bullets: High icons = protection from demands + stronger demands of your own
Low icons = vulnerable to attack
Splaying is the fastest way to boost icon counts
Watch opponents' threatening demand cards and their featured icons

summary.title: Splay Whenever You Can
bullets: Splay left = 1 extra icon, right = 2 extra, up = 3 extra per card
Even small splays compound over time as stacks grow
A splayed stack of 4-5 cards is a defensive fortress
Always take splay opportunities from dogma effects seriously

summary.title: Do Not Hoard Cards in Your Hand
bullets: Hand cards have no icons, no effects, and score no points
Meld them or score them — do not let them sit idle
More than 2-3 cards in hand usually means you are hoarding
Exception: saving cards from demand theft

summary.title: Advance Through the Ages
bullets: Higher-age cards = stronger effects and more score value
Your draw level matches your highest top card age
Stay at or near the leading age among players
Falling behind in age means weaker draws

summary.title: Respect the Late Game
bullets: Age 9-10 cards can win instantly or reset the entire board
If leading, close out before age 9-10 volatility
If behind, racing to late game may be your comeback path
Forcing a draw past age 10 ends on score — valid if score is high

walkthrough.title: The Icon Economy
walkthrough.text: Advanced Innovation play revolves around the icon economy. Every demand and every sharing decision depends on relative icon counts. The best players do not just react to icon counts; they manipulate them. They splay to boost their own counts, cover opponents' key icons by forcing transfers, and choose which cards to meld based on how it changes the icon balance. Think of icons as a currency. Splaying is like printing money. Demand effects are like taxing opponents. The player who controls the icon economy controls the game.

walkthrough.title: Aggressive Demand Strategies
walkthrough.text: Some of the most powerful cards in Innovation are demand cards. Archery, Oars, and Metalworking in age 1 can devastate opponents early. In later ages, cards like Engineering, Gunpowder, and Pirate Code transfer score pile cards or destroy board position. The key to a demand strategy is maintaining a higher count of the featured icon than your targets. Splay the matching icon type aggressively, and time your demands for maximum disruption — ideally right after an opponent scores points but before they can achieve.

walkthrough.title: The Splay Arms Race
walkthrough.text: Splaying is the most impactful non-scoring action in Innovation. A stack of four cards splayed up reveals twelve extra icons. That kind of icon count makes you immune to almost every demand in the game. Advanced players race to splay key colors as early as possible. Left splays are available from age 1 and 2 cards. Right splays start appearing around age 4. Up splays come in the later ages and are the most powerful. Prioritize dogma effects that offer splays, even over scoring opportunities. The icon advantage from a splay often yields more points over subsequent turns than a single score action.

walkthrough.title: Score Pile Management
walkthrough.text: Your score pile serves two purposes: it is the threshold for achievements, and it is the tiebreaker if the game ends by drawing past age 10. Score high-value cards whenever possible. An age 6 card in your score pile is worth six age 1 cards. Do not waste scoring opportunities on low-value cards if you can help it. At the same time, protect your score pile from demands like Pirate Code that transfer cards out. If your score dips below an achievement threshold, you lose the ability to claim it. Keep mental track of how many points you need for the next achievement.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Achievements
walkthrough.text: Claiming achievements is almost always correct, but timing matters. Sometimes it is better to claim a higher achievement first if a lower one is safe. If no opponent can reach the age 5 achievement but the age 3 is contested, grab the 3 first. In other cases, leaping to a higher achievement denies opponents the satisfaction of catching up. Also consider the action cost: each achievement costs one of your two actions. If you need two achievements on the same turn but can only claim one, pick the one your opponents are closest to stealing.

walkthrough.title: Special Achievement Racing
walkthrough.text: Special achievements are a parallel victory path that experienced players exploit. Monument rewards tucking or scoring six cards in one turn — certain combo-heavy cards make this achievable. Empire requires three of all six icon types, which is achievable with good splaying. World needs twelve clock icons, which is realistic in the mid-to-late game with splayed stacks. Wonder requires five colors all splayed up or right, which is hard but game-winning. Universe needs five top cards all age 8 or higher. Watch for opportunities to pick these up alongside normal achievements.

walkthrough.title: Reading Opponents' Boards
walkthrough.text: Innovation is not solitaire. Pay close attention to what your opponents are building. What are their top cards? Which demands threaten you? What are their icon counts? Can they achieve this turn? If an opponent is one achievement from winning, you need to either claim that achievement yourself, attack their score pile, or end the game through age 10 before they can act. Spending a few seconds reading the board state before your turn is the difference between a reactive player and a strategic one.

walkthrough.title: The Free Draw Engine
walkthrough.text: Advanced players deliberately engineer sharing situations to generate free draws. If you have a non-demand effect and an opponent has enough icons to share, dogma-ing that card gives you the effect plus a free draw. This effectively turns your two-action turn into a three-action turn. Over the course of a game, generating five or six free draws gives you a significant tempo advantage. Build your icon counts to be comparable to opponents rather than overwhelming them, so sharing triggers consistently.

walkthrough.title: The Age 10 Endgame
walkthrough.text: When the game reaches age 9 and 10, the rules change. Cards at this level can end the game instantly. If you are behind on achievements, pushing the game into age 10 territory is a valid Hail Mary. If the age 10 pile runs out and someone would draw an 11, the game ends on score. You can deliberately accelerate this by drawing and dogma-ing high-age cards that burn through the supply. Conversely, if you are ahead on achievements, slow down. Do not draw from age 10 unless you must. Control the pace and close out with your remaining achievement targets.

walkthrough.title: Adapting to Chaos
walkthrough.text: Innovation is inherently chaotic. With 105 unique cards and dramatic swings in board state, no two games play the same way. The best players embrace this instead of fighting it. They do not commit to a rigid plan but instead evaluate the best tactical option each turn. If a powerful card lands in your hand that does not match your current strategy, consider pivoting. If an opponent's demand wrecks your score pile, look for a new path forward instead of dwelling on what was lost. Flexibility and opportunism win more games than rigid planning. The player who best adapts to the ever-shifting board state is the one who claims that final achievement.

summary.title: The Icon Economy
bullets: Icons are the currency of Innovation — they govern demands and sharing
Splay to boost your own counts, force transfers to reduce opponents'
The player who controls the icon economy controls the game

Maintain higher featured icon count than targets for your demand cards
Time demands right after opponents score but before they achieve

bullets: Splay left (ages 1-2) < splay right (ages 4+) < splay up (late ages)
4 cards splayed up = 12 extra icons — near-total demand immunity

summary.title: Timing Your Achievements
bullets: Grab contested achievements first, save safe ones for later
Sometimes claim a higher achievement first to deny opponents
Each achievement costs 1 action — plan your 2-action turn around it
If 2 achievements are available, take the one opponents are closest to

summary.title: Special Achievement Racing

Empire: 3+ of all 6 icons — achievable with good splay management
World: 12+ clocks — realistic in mid-late game with splayed stacks
Wonder: 5 colors splayed up/right — hard but game-winning
Universe: 5 top cards all age 8+ — late game objective

summary.title: Reading Opponents' Boards
bullets: Check opponents' top cards, icon counts, and score before your turn
Identify which demands threaten you and which you can exploit
If an opponent is 1 achievement from winning, disrupt immediately
Spending a few seconds reading the board separates good from great

summary.title: The Free Draw Engine
bullets: Engineer sharing to turn 2-action turns into 3-action turns
Keep icon counts comparable to opponents (not overwhelming) for consistent sharing
5-6 free draws per game = massive tempo advantage
Deliberately dogma non-demand effects when opponents will share

summary.title: The Age 10 Endgame
bullets: Behind on achievements? Push to age 10 — force a score-based ending
Ahead on achievements? Slow down — avoid burning the age 10 pile
Drawing past age 10 = game ends immediately, highest score wins
Control the pace to match your win condition

summary.title: Adapting to Chaos
bullets: 105 unique cards = no two games are the same
Evaluate the best tactical option each turn, not a rigid plan
Pivot if a powerful off-strategy card lands in your hand
Flexibility and opportunism win more than rigid planning

entries.definition: One of the four actions. Take the top card from the supply pile matching the age of your highest top card on your board. If no cards are on your board, draw from age 1. The card goes into your hand.
One of the four actions. Play a card from your hand onto your board, on top of any existing cards of the same color. Continues any existing splay. You can meld any card regardless of age.
One of the four actions and the core mechanic of Innovation. Activate one of your top cards to execute its effects. Involves icon counting, sharing, and demand resolution.
One of the four actions. Claim a normal achievement if your score is at least 5 times its age number and you have a top card of equal or higher age. Points are not spent.
A dogma effect in a darker box starting with 'I demand.' Targets opponents with fewer of the featured icon than you. Resolved clockwise. Players with equal or more icons are immune.
Any dogma effect that is not a demand. Shared by opponents who have at least as many of the featured icon as you. They resolve clockwise before you.
When opponents execute your non-demand effects because they have equal or more of the featured icon. If any sharing causes a game change, you earn one free Draw action.
The small icon shown next to each dogma effect on a card. Determines who shares non-demand effects and who is immune to demand effects based on icon count comparison.
A bonus Draw action earned when any opponent shares one of your non-demand effects and causes a game change (cards drawn, melded, scored, tucked, splayed, exchanged, moved, or revealed).
A special tag on some dogma effects. These effects cannot be shared by opponents regardless of icon count, though they can still impact other players.
Fan the cards in a color stack to the left. Reveals one additional icon per covered card (the bottom-left icon). Available from early-age card effects.
Fan the cards in a color stack to the right. Reveals two additional icons per covered card (the bottom-right and bottom-left icons). Appears from mid-age card effects.
Fan the cards in a color stack upward. Reveals three additional icons per covered card (all three bottom icons). The most powerful splay direction. Appears on late-age cards.
The default state of a color stack. Only the top card's icons are visible. A stack with zero or one cards is always unsplayed. Applying a new splay replaces the old one.
One of the six icon types. Appears frequently on early-age cards (ages 1-3). Used as the featured icon on several demand and non-demand effects.
One of the six icon types. Appears on early and mid-age cards. Used as a featured icon for various dogma effects.
One of the six icon types. Appears on early and mid-age cards. Writing's dogma uses the lightbulb as its featured icon. Common in blue cards.

One of the six icon types. Prominent in early ages (1-3). Featured icon on Archery and City States. Can become a liability later if not covered, since cards like Gunpowder and Engineering target castle-heavy players.
One of the six icon types. Begins appearing around age 4 and becomes dominant in mid-to-late ages. Many powerful mid-game effects use factory as their featured icon.
One of the six icon types. Appears on later-age cards. Twelve or more visible clocks on your board lets you claim the World special achievement.
The hexagonal image on each card depicting the innovation. Occupies one of the four icon positions but provides no gameplay benefit. Its position affects which splay direction best reveals icons.
A collection of face-down cards placed under your reference card. Each card is worth points equal to its age number. Your total score is the sum of all ages in your score pile.
Your play area containing up to five color stacks of melded cards. Only top cards can be activated for Dogma. Visible icons on your entire board determine sharing and demand immunity.
Cards you have drawn but not yet played. Hand cards have no icons or effects. They can be melded, or used/lost through dogma effects. You can always look at your own hand.
The ten face-down piles of cards (ages 1-10) in the center of the table. When you draw, you take from the pile matching your highest top card age. If empty, draw from next higher pile.
To score a card means to place it face down in your score pile. It is now worth points equal to its age number. Scored cards lose all icons and effects.
Place a card on the bottom of its matching color stack on your board. Continues any existing splay. If no cards of that color exist on your board, it starts a new stack.
Place a card at the bottom of its matching age supply pile, face down. If multiple cards are returned at once, choose the order. If the pile was empty, it now exists again.
Swap cards between two specified locations. Still takes effect even if one side of the exchange is empty. Exchanging does not count as scoring for the Monument special achievement.
Draw a card from the specified age supply pile, then immediately meld it onto your board. A compound instruction treated as one action within a dogma effect.
Draw a card from the specified age supply pile, then immediately tuck it at the bottom of its matching color stack. A compound instruction within a dogma effect.
Draw a card from the specified age supply pile, then immediately place it in your score pile. A compound instruction within a dogma effect.
The fully visible card on top of a color stack. Only top cards can be activated with the Dogma action. Your highest top card age determines your draw level.
The card at the bottom of a color stack. If a stack has only one card, it is both the top and bottom card.
A card's value is its age number (1-10). If an effect references the value of something you do not have (e.g., your top purple card when you have no purple), treat the value as 0.
Refers to the age value of the cards in question. In case of a tie, the player affected can choose which of their tied cards is affected.
Used by the Fission card. Removed cards are set aside in the box and out of the game entirely. Can force all players to lose their hands, score piles, and board cards.
Several late-age cards can end the game immediately. Effects that say 'the single player with the most X wins' only work if there is no tie for X. If tied, the effect is ignored entirely.
The earliest era. Cards include Archery, City States, Clothing, Code of Laws, Metalworking, Mysticism, Oars, Pottery, The Wheel, and Writing. Heavy in castle and crown icons.
The second era. Features innovations like Mathematics, Philosophy, Monotheism, Road Building, and Construction. Introduces early left-splay effects and the first real scoring engines.
The third era. Innovations include Machinery, Education, Feudalism, Engineering, Compass, Paper, Alchemy, and Translation. Demands become more aggressive. Engineering punishes castle-heavy boards.
The fourth era. Features Printing Press, Gunpowder, Navigation, Anatomy, Perspective, Invention, and Reformation. Factory icons begin appearing. Right-splay effects emerge.
The fifth era. Innovations include Astronomy, Measurement, Statistics, Steam Engine, Coal, The Pirate Code, and Banking. Scoring potential increases significantly. Astronomy can claim Universe.
The sixth era. Features Canning, Vaccination, Classification, Emancipation, Democracy, and Industrialization. Powerful scoring and splaying effects. Emancipation offers right-splay.
The seventh era. Innovations include Lighting, Railroad, Refrigeration, Bicycle, Evolution, Publications, and Combustion. Cards become increasingly powerful with game-altering effects.
The eighth era. Features Rocketry, Flight, Mobility, Mass Media, Antibiotics, Skyscrapers, and Corporations. Very powerful demands and scoring effects. Board states can shift dramatically.
The ninth era. Innovations include Computers, Genetics, Composites, Fission, Ecology, Suburbia, and Services. Some cards can end the game immediately. Fission can remove all cards from the game.

The final era. Features A.I., The Internet, Databases, Robotics, Bioengineering, Software, Miniaturization, Globalization, Stem Cells, and Self Service. Extremely powerful effects that often end the game outright.

Special achievement. Claim immediately if you tuck six cards or score six cards during a single turn. Transferred cards from opponents and exchanged cards do not count. Also claimable via Masonry (Age 1).
Special achievement. Claim immediately if you have three or more visible icons of all six types (crown, leaf, lightbulb, castle, factory, clock). Also claimable via Construction (Age 2).
Special achievement. Claim immediately if you have twelve or more clock icons visible on your board. Also claimable via Translation (Age 3).
Special achievement. Claim immediately if you have all five colors on your board and each is splayed either up or right. Also claimable via Invention (Age 4).
Special achievement. Claim immediately if you have five top cards, each of value 8 or higher. Also claimable via Astronomy (Age 5).

One of nine face-down cards (ages 1-9) set aside during setup. Claimed via the Achieve action when you have score >= 5x the age and a top card >= that age. First-come-first-served; cannot be stolen once claimed.
One of the five card colors. Red cards tend to feature aggressive demand effects, particularly in early ages (Archery, Metalworking, Oars).
One of the five card colors. Blue cards often feature draw and knowledge-related effects (Writing, Mathematics, Astronomy).
One of the five card colors. Green cards include economic and infrastructure innovations (Clothing, The Wheel, Agriculture, Compass).
One of the five card colors. Yellow cards feature civilization-building effects related to production and society (Pottery, Fermenting, Industrialization).
One of the five card colors. Purple cards often involve governance, religion, and social structures (Code of Laws, Mysticism, Philosophy, Democracy).

If a supply pile is empty and you need to draw from it, draw from the next higher non-empty pile instead. This applies to all draws, including Draw actions and dogma effects. If you must draw higher than age 10, the game ends immediately.

entries.term: Do As Much As You Can
entries.definition: If you cannot fully carry out a dogma effect, perform as much of it as possible and ignore the rest. A demand for your three highest cards takes your whole hand if you have only two. An exchange still occurs if one side is empty.
If two players become eligible for a special achievement at exactly the same time, the tie is broken in turn order going clockwise, with the current player winning ties.

Four-player variant with two teams of two. Teammates do not demand each other. Sharing with only your teammate does not earn a free draw. Opponent effects skip your teammate. Team needs 6 combined achievements to win.
In a 2- or 3-player game, the first player takes only 1 action on their opening turn. In a 4-player game, the first two players each take only 1 action. All subsequent turns are 2 actions.
